comments.title: Predicting WW2
comments.content:
In The Twilight of Pluto, you explain that a group of British astrologers announced in 1939 that there would be no war that year. (And of course--spoiler alert--they were wrong.) In a footnote you say there were obvious indications of war which these astrologers misread because of their political prejudices. Can you say more about why they got it so badly wrong? What did they misread, and why?

Thanks very much!

replies.title: Re: Predicting WW2
replies.content: My forthcoming book on mundane astrology covers that in great detail. The short form is that they failed to notice a hard square between Mars and an angular Moon -- a major traditional signal for war -- and they also failed to notice a mutual reception between Mars and Saturn, which gave both planets a great deal of strength. As for why -- well, one of the ugly secrets of English society before the Second World War is that a lot of people were highly sympathetic to Nazi Germany, and several leading astrologers (including Charles E.O. Carter, the head of the group we're discussing) were among them. In his editorial in the issue of an astrology mag that was on the newsstands when war broke out, he insisted that there should be no hostility at all between Britain and Germany because, ahem, they were so racially similar...

replies.title: Re: Predicting WW2
replies.content:
Charles still seems to have boosters as there is a web-site dedicated to his memory.

https://www.charlescarter.co.uk/

Of interest to anyone who knows their way around natal charts, his birth chart is shown on the homepage.

JLfromNH/Turquoise Disoriented Tick

replies.title: Re: Predicting WW2
replies.content: Carter's work more broadly is worth attending to -- he was a very capable natal astrologer and wrote some useful books. It was apparently just in mundane astrology that he let his opinions get in the way of paying attention to the heavens.

Hi JMG -

Do divinations 'expire'? In other words is there a window of time that I can successfully follow through on the result? For example, if I get a positive horary reading for relocating to a certain place, is that reading good until I'm able to make the move, regardless of how long it might take?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It really varies. Some divinations seem to be good for the long term, and others are not. I recommend that your question should always include a time element to take this into account: e.g., "Should I relocate to such-and-such a place in the next year?"

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: If I can piggy-back off this, what’s your personal process for relocation readings? I can read astrological charts (working on some of Chris Warnock’s courses but just partway through the Astro Magic course)? Do you use Horary? I thought I recalled you mentioning another method in a past post.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I use a method I got from one of Llewellyn George's books. His approach was to cast your natal chart as though you had been born at the place you're considering relocating to -- exactly the same moment, corrected for changes in time zone, but from the different place. That means all the planets will be in the identical points in the zodiac but, if you're moving any distance, in different houses. The advantage here is that you can move malefics out of houses where they give you problems and into houses where they're irrelevant, while moving benefics into angular positions and important houses. Imagine you have a well-dignified Jupiter in your 12th house, where it does you very little good; if you move east, you may be able to get it into your first house, where it will give you good luck, and if you move west, you may be able to get it into your 10th and have great success in your career. That's the kind of thing you can do with George's technique, and in my experience it works very well.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: At most times of year, the house cusps will be very different.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
To furnish an example and add on to this, I ran my own chart as if I had been born roughly 1000 miles due north of where I actually was.

The midheaven and nadir stay put in Placidus (and in other quadrant-house systems), but my ascendant goes from 9 Capricorn to 1 Capricorn (and my descendant from 9 Cancer to 1 Cancer). In my case, this change isn't immaterial; it moves Venus and Neptune from the 12th house into the 1st, and if I had been born ten minutes or so earlier, it would have made me a Sagittarius rising ruled by Jupiter instead of a Capricorn rising ruled by Saturn.

(If you use equal-house or whole-sign, those systems are based on the ascendant, which changes with latitude, meaning even your midheaven and nadir will change.)

replies.content:
I did a geomantic reading that told me very clearly not to quit an addiction (Fortuna Major in the first house, Rubeus in twelfth house, denial of perfection). I then asked, "should I keep practicing magic even though I get stoned at night?" (I do my practices only when I am not high, although I question whether I should practice at all, given that there are effects on the way one's mind works if one gets stoned every day that are present even when one is not actively high.) In response to that question, I got a chart with Caput Draconis in the first house and Fortuna Minor in the eighth house, but no mode of perfection.

My assumption was that favorable figures tell you you should do something, and that a mode of perfection tells you it will work. It seems unimaginable that this is saying "keep getting stoned and stop doing your practices." So my next thought was, "Since I didn't ask about whether an endeavor will work, are modes of perfection pertinent in this chart?" I was considering that divination answers questions with inhuman literality.

Then there is the question of whether a daily protective ritual is "magic," and whether this is not saying "stop the Middle Pillar exercise," but rather "don't do anything past that." I am aware that all this may be saying exactly what I don't want it to say. I have also had problems with the Watcher at the Threshold, though, and recently attempted to quit getting high, but my sleep problems came back, and the clarity I experienced in my morning practices the day after quitting weed went away, my visualizations became low-quality again, and so I ate some edibles last night.

In particular, I got off track with my practices when I visited my evangelical Christian folks for the holidays, one of whom seemed suspicious around me and asked what I was doing in my room. So I am probably being prayed for, to the effect that I stop doing these things. I just cannot wrap my head around it all at the moment.

replies.title: (no subject)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I think that I see what Ataulfo is asking. Should vs will. If ask 'will x buy my house' a mode of perfection gives a yes answer and the figure indicates something about the outcome, for example laetitia indicates I will be happy about it and tristitia indicates I will be sad about it.

But 'should I sell my car to x' with a mode of perfection indicates that yes, I should, that it is for the best even if it perfects with tristitia.

I think that the issue here is 'should' perfecting with an unfavorable figure. In a 'will x' question perfection gives a yes and the lack there of gives a no, and the figure gives the favorability of the action. But for a 'should' question the perfection gives a yes, ie favorable, and a negative figure indicates unfavorable.

I think Ataulfo is overthinking this and conflating it a bit. For a 'should' question the perfection is perceived as vague because the 'should' is vague. Should according to who? The law, my peers, what will make me happy, what will make me grow, it will clear karma but I will hate it, etc.

So Ataulfo is asking for clarification on how to interpret a 'should I do x' question that perfects with an unfavorable figure.

I would suggest either asking a simple and direct question that doesn't suffer from this vagueness issue, and the asking follow up questions if any, or phrasing the question in suck a way that the nature of the 'should' is made explicit. Does that sound aboyt right JMG?

comments.title: Effective Goals for Prayer
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

It’s been quite pleasant to watch the Ecosophia Prayer List attract a group of steady regulars, as well as a fair amount of prayer requests, including some directly to the list. Once you find the time to get the Intentional Prayer Network materials dusted off and ready for action, I think there will already be a core of pray-ers in place who will likely be able to get the engine running in short order.

My question is about the subject matter of prayers. The vast majority of our prayer requests are health related, which makes sense given the List’s origin in the Ecosophia Covid threads. The next most common categories are requests for spiritually aided luck in some form, whether that mean a smooth birth, finding a better home, or in one case, improving one’s financial state. There is also the category of spiritual protection.

I presume in all of these cases that success or failure of the prayers will in large part also depend on the initiative of the people making the requests. I.e., people requesting prayers for health but acting in ways harmful to their condition, or people requesting help finding new lodging but unwilling to take positive steps to search for this themselves, will be rather less likely to meet with success, even if a legion of monks is chanting daily for their success.

There is one more potential category which occurs to me, which has been hinted at in a couple of requests but hasn’t really been requested explicitly yet, with one single exception. This would be prayer requests for aid with personal development. In this category I would put prayer requests for help with personal issues that are ultimately the final responsibility of the supplicant: requests for help in breaking addictions, changing bad habits, forming good ones, and taking onboard important new changes of perspective. In your opinion and experience, would requesting prayer towards aid with these kinds of ends be helpful? As long as the one making the request is also making good faith efforts to effect that change themselves, to the best of their ability (even though they clearly haven’t been successful on their own yet)?

replies.title: Re: Effective Goals for Prayer
replies.content: Yes, exactly. Prayer won't do the work for you, but it'll give you a boost in the right direction. If you're already trying to move in the right direction, as in work for personal development, prayer can help quite a bit; if you're doing your best to move in the other direction and expect prayer to make up for it, you're going to be disappointed.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Sigh.

One of the things that every teacher of occultism learns early on is that there are certain categories of problem students that show up again and again. One of the most common is the student who insists on always tinkering with the practices -- there's always this detail or that detail that they don't like, for whatever reason, and if you tell them that the practices are the way they are for good reason, they just come back with more arguments for why they ought to do it differently.

The secret behind this habit is that it's a defense mechanism the ego uses to protect itself against the personal growth and change that comes with occult practice. Such students never actually make any progress until they get over themselves, do the practices exactly as given, and learn and grow as a result.

I think you can intuit my advice from the above...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Why does the ego generally resist occult-related growth and development? Is the answer related to attachment to the material plane?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The ego is your self-concept. It's what you imagine yourself to be. Most people invest a great deal of emotional energy in their self-concepts, and so anything that threatens to change the self-concept runs up against the psychology of previous investment. "This is what I am! I can't possibly be anything different!" Yet the ego is always at least a little inaccurate, and most often wildly inaccurate, so people are caught up in a constant struggle to defend a false self-concept against the threat of realizing that it's false. Occult practices, if you pursue them seriously, make it impossible to prop up the charade indefinitely, so the resistance to change is always an issue. Fortunately, since the basic practices are relatively gentle in their effects, they help you to dismantle the false self-concept a little at a time and put something more accurate and (importantly) more flexible in its place.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Hm!

So a person could create an entire system of spirituality that is really just a mirror for the ego to stare at… as it were.

And this is less likely when simply accepting an established system as developed by a trustworthy source.

I certainly have experienced decades of wasted time by practicing ‘looking-glass spirituality’. ;-)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Most systems of pop-culture spirituality are exactly that sort of ego-mirroring exercise -- that's why they're so popular, but it's also why they vanish without a trace after a few decades, once people get bored with going through the motions. An established system will doubtless have its problems, but it won't simply mirror yours back at you!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Pride comes from loving yourself but not loving anyone else. As long as you make sure to direct the same unconditional love at others you direct toward yourself, you'll be fime.

2) The earth energies aren't limitless, but they'll remain available as long as the sun continues to shine -- etheric energy, including the energy in the earth currents, is another form of solar power, and can be expected to keep flowing until the sun dies a couple of billion years from now. Egypt has been a desert since the rain belts shifted about 6,000 years ago; Greece became a desert after the Mycenean period and then recovered due to the temple technology and the practice of an ancient system of permaculture that relied on olive trees, grapevines, and sacred groves in the right places. (Greece could be green again if the old traditions were put back to use.) The earth currents are by no means foolproof, and you can mess things up badly if you misuse them, but they're sustainable in human terms.

1) If those actually existed -- the jury's still out, though there's some evidence for the Atlantean and Lemurian cycles of civilization -- the dates and locations are tentative at best. The civilizations of the Polarian and Hyperborean cycles had their homelands in the far north, and those almost certainly existed before the last ice age, when the Arctic Ocean was ice-free. The Lemurian cycle had its most influential civilization in a now-drowned subcontinent that geologists today call Sundaland, south and east of southeast Asia; it went under rising seas around 14,000 BC. The Atlantean cycle, the most recent and best documented, seems to have had its homeland around the North Atlantic coasts when the sea was a couple of hundred feet lower than it is now, and it was drowned around 9600 BC.

2) Magic was apparently much practiced in Atlantean times. Other than that, we have no clue.

3) Nobody knows.

4) Nobody knows.

replies.title: Re: Swords and major arcana

To a very large extent tarot readings depend on the person who's doing them, and my interpretation of your reading is thus not likely to tell you much. comments.title: meditating on love
comments.content:
Hi JMG

Further to my ramblings on the Essene blog
https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/217157.html?thread=38618949#cmt38618949
I've spent the last week pondering 'unconditional love' and a couple of things came up.
Something I often do when thinking about a subject is to randomly open a book of Sufi aphorisms and stories - here is what I opened the page to after meditating on unconditional love and my thoughts about the matter:-

A man went to Wahab Imri and said:
'Teach me humility.'
Wahab replied:
'I cannot do that, because humility is a teacher of itself. It is learnt by means of its practice. If you cannot practice it, you cannot learn it. If you cannot learn it, you do not really want to learn it inwardly at all.'

That gave me a bit of a lightbulb moment - replace the word 'humility' with 'unconditional love'

The phrase 'unconditional love' I have seen misused repeatedly and that had tainted my view - but my view was completely wrong because I had not been thinking of unconditional love but the people seemingly misusing the term... completely different things - first, the words are not the thing, second, a concept is independent of the people using it, and thirdly, thank heavens for the local robins (see below).

Another error on my part was to try and put love into a pigeonhole - granted I know that there are different kinds of love, but I was making things too complicated. So, I stepped back and thought why should it be a problem to feel love of the divine just because I do not have the words to describe it intellectually?
Since I do not know 'what' the divine islet alone the words to describe such a thing, then it is not a problem to not be able to describe love (of the divine).

Anyway, this morning the resident robin spotted me and waited in one of the usual spots for me to go and give mealworms (this has been going on for over 8 years) - my ponderings on love were in mind and the robin is an expression of the divine and somehow we and this bird have formed a relationship and I suddenly discovered that if I considered the robin, his mate, and the local blackbird who comes to feed with them, I suddenly had an answer to my question. :)

There are not words and that is delightfully irrelevant!

So that prompted me to post, and, to cement the point home (the universe tapping my thick skull), looking for the Wahab Imri text above, I picked up the wrong book and the page I opened to gave me:

"Love is a Way to Truth, to Knowledge, to Action. But only those who know of real love can approach these things by means of love. The others have misunderstood certain other feelings for those of real love."

:)

PS If you have a reference/link for that medieval Zen stuff, I'd like to read more if there is something in English.

Thanks

[earthworm]

1) It's a little more complex than that. Magic is a specific set of disciplines meant to have certain effects on the practitioner and/or the world. Occultism is a much broader field of study which can include magic, but doesn't have to. Occultism, as the book suggests, is the science of the Unseen, while magic is a particular set of skills that allow you to do certain things with the Unseen. Thus there are occultists who practice magic and occultists who don't. The Golden Section training doesn't include magic, though it doesn't forbid it either: it provides a good solid training in general occultism.

2) That's a good point.

replies.title: Re: Animal Spirits
replies.content:
There's an urban myth that Benjamin Franklin officially proposed for the national animal to be the turkey. It's an exaggeration; his proposal for the Great Seal didn't have any birds at all, but he thought the version of the design on a medal for the Cincinnati, a society of officers from the revolution, looked like a turkey and thought that was to be preferred to the bald eagle it was supposed to be. From a letter to his daughter:

"For my own part I wish the Bald Eagle had not been chosen the Representative of our Country. He is a Bird of bad moral Character. He does not get his Living honestly. You may have seen him perched on some dead Tree near the River, where, too lazy to fish for himself, he watches the Labour of the Fishing Hawk; and when that diligent Bird has at length taken a Fish, and is bearing it to his Nest for the Support of his Mate and young Ones, the Bald Eagle pursues him and takes it from him.

"With all this Injustice, he is never in good Case but like those among Men who live by Sharping & Robbing he is generally poor and often very lousy. Besides he is a rank Coward: The little King Bird not bigger than a Sparrow attacks him boldly and drives him out of the District. He is therefore by no means a proper Emblem for the brave and honest Cincinnati of America who have driven all the King birds from our Country...

"I am on this account not displeased that the Figure is not known as a Bald Eagle, but looks more like a Turkey. For the Truth the Turkey is in Comparison a much more respectable Bird, and withal a true original Native of America... He is besides, though a little vain & silly, a Bird of Courage, and would not hesitate to attack a Grenadier of the British Guards who should presume to invade his Farm Yard with a red Coat on."

https://www.greatseal.com/symbols/turkey.html

"His 1775 letter in the Pennsylvania Journal made a good case for the Rattlesnake as an appropriate symbol of 'the temper and conduct of America.'"

If we'd officially switched animals once it was clear we couldn't expect to continue doing even only as a good of a job of being an imperial power as we had been, before or shortly after the second Iraq war, we might have saved some trouble.

You have the option of deciding the to put the turkey first for yourself. (Probably wild turkeys rather than domesticated; they're more cunning.) Or a rattlesnake. Would this put you out of synch with the current national egregor, for better or worse?

comments.title: The Heart Sphere of Protection
comments.content:
As you well know, buried in one of the Modern Order of Essenes lessons is a very simple, early version of  Sphere of Protection, apparently created by Reverend Matthew Shaw. I have taken to calling it "The Heart SOP", and I've taken the liberty of drawing it out of the lesson and putting it on its own page here, along with some notes and reflections on it. 

I would like to nominate the Heart SOP as being useful enough to get its own post at some point on your Dreamwidth site. There are a few reasons why. 

(a) It is the only protection ritual you've shared which, even in its "official" form, can be performed correctly even by someone with severe physical disabilities, paralysis, or injuries. In fact I have already recommended it to a disabled friend on that basis.

(b) It seems to be completely safe around all children, even unusually sensitive ones. This is an issue that I don't propose to get into any detail here right now, but I'll just say that I have ceased to trust the more modern versions of the SOP as being safe around young children, though you used to assert that it was-- though, to be fair, I haven't heard you say that for some time now. My divination also indicates it to be safe to do in the same general vicinity as my kids, which was not the case for the modern SOP.

(c) It is completely religiously compatible with just about any faith I can think of, save the most diehard prejudiced versions of them. Of course that's true of all SOP's, but it's much more obviously, immediately apparent in this case.

(d) There's also the matter that there is a much shorter learning curve to learning it, and performing it takes much less time than a full modern SOP or comparable ritual.

(e) Initial indications seem to suggest markedly beneficial effects. I am not actually sure how effective it is as a protection ritual just yet-- I know that you said that you practiced it enough to make sure that it functions well as a protection ritual, but you have so many decades of experience at a variety of daily protection rituals that I suspect you could easily turn the daily ritual of eating a ham sandwich into effective spiritual protection. But other reported data points so far are:

     ♡ Marked sensations of wellbeing and ease.
     ♡ A state of intense focus.
     ♡ A strengthening of one's spiritual heart organ.
     ♡ A sense of imperviousness to criticism for at least one overly sensitive vegetarian.
     ♡ Increased ability to access feelings of joy.
     ♡ Increased ability to connect with music while performing.
     ♡ Incidence of heart related synchronicities.

So. Question time.

(1) COULD you turn the daily ritual of eating a ham sandwich into effective spiritual protection? ;-) Okay, that one's not really serious. Though to be honest I do kind of want to know.

(2) On first principles it seems to me that the Heart SOP would be weaker as a protection ritual compared to other rituals, due to the fact that there is only the movement of the breath to link the ritual to the material plane. At the moment it seems, in terms of physical movements, exactly as active as the "pure astral" forms of other protective ritual. Is there something else about the Heart SOP that compensates for this, or perhaps do I have the wrong idea about how this works?

(3) Although I expect you haven't divined on the matter, from your knowledge of how these things work, do you think my assertion that this version of the SOP would be particularly safe around children compared to other rituals seems reasonable?

(4) One of the commenters on my Heart SOP post said that he'd been practicing it not at the heart, but at the "heart center", on the central vertical axis. Would you expect this to have roughly the same effect as visualizing at the heart? Benefits, drawbacks? I do have to say that envisioning the growing sphere off center has caused me minor difficulties.

(5) Did the data points I cited above sound to you like reasonably possible effects of performing this ritual?

(6) Given its utter simplicity, and though I understand it would take testing to be sure, do you think that there's a chance that it might interact well even in combination with other modes of energetic ritual, such as the Middle Pillar or the like?

I thank you for any answers you may offer to this multipart comment. I do not normally post long questions like this, so I hope you will forgive me this once. :-)

replies.title: Re: The Heart Sphere of Protection
replies.content: One further data point I forgot to mention, and one last small question if I may: divination suggested I not do this ritual when i was in a state of very mild excitement purely due to drinking two coffees two or three hours earlier. I guess because my heart was racing a little faster than usual. So it seems there are definite limitations to bear in mind. Do you have any insight as to why one might ought to avoid caffeine before doing the Heart SOP? Is there any mild danger risk that you can foresee in the matter?

replies.title: Re: The Heart Sphere of Protection
replies.content:
1) Yes, if you do it with intention.

2) Protective rituals are like martial arts: systems don't compete, only individuals do. Somebody who for whatever reason was well suited to Shaw's SOP might get better results with it than with a different ritual.

3) I would expect it to be equally safe.

4) Experiment and find out!

5) Yes.

6) Impossible to tell without extensive experimentation. If you'd like to volunteer, by all means.

comments.title: Diagnostic SOP
comments.content:
I performed an SOP this weekend and had distinct trouble visualizing the sphere of light at the end. I realized that the problem had happened in the past in this same location (not my normal SOP location.) In the past I did my best and moved on. This time I waited and listened, and then realized that I didn't feel safe, balanced, nor strong in this location. At first I was vexed and upset, thinking "the SOP is supposed to MAKE me safe and more in balance."

Sitting with it longer, I realized that SOP is a tool and will only do what it does in the hands of a capable operator - the SOP wasn't malfunctioning, instead I had lost the will and focus to use it properly. I then formed an affirmation confirming that I possess and direct my power and my light (in all spaces.) Afterward the SOP functioned complete with closing.

This helps me to see the SOP in a new light - that it functions as a protective working, and when it does not it is a clear diagnoses of MY state.

It feels like this alines with the second Essene Healer lesson. When I first read it, at first I recoiled a bit at the language that felt like "the law of attraction" and "the secret" as I've watched them be misused to excuse bad behavior. Freedom/bondage being particular triggers as there are forms of physical bondage that can't be thought out of. I've re-read it in light of my SOP experience and am sensing in it that they are referring to internal states and rather than prescribing "manifesting" your way out of your problems, they are more giving a diagnostic tool: when you feel yourself in bondage or in anger, you can take that info and do things with it, rather than feeling powerless from it.

Does this all make sense within the intentions for these systems as you understand them?

Thank you for this space and your counsel. :)

replies.title: Re: Diagnostic SOP
replies.content: Excellent! Yes, this is one of the ways that a regular ritual working becomes useful.

replies.title: Re: Diagnostic SOP
replies.content: Thank you. I'm finding more and more confirmation of your recommendation to perform a regular ritual.

comments.title: Late/Incomplete Personality Development & Dolmen Arch's "Who is Kim?"
comments.content:
1. Thank you for recommending Schopenhauer's World as Will & Representation! I've ordered it and look forward to diving in.

2. I'm on the completion exercise for Dolman Arch Grade 3 of "Who is Kim, Kim, Kim?" As suggested it does cause a shift in consciousness, but I have a question about identifying with oneself and the personality.

I've been doing a lot of examination of my stressful upbringing, aided by Patrick Teahan's youtube channel on processing childhood trauma. In my case I was not beaten because I figured out exactly what my unbalanced parent wanted at all times and danced the perfect dance of walking on eggshells. I was an empty doll, a mirror, not allowed to become my own person or have my own opinions. I had difficulty recognizing myself in photos until well into my twenties.

Parsing my likes and dislikes as an aspect of my personality, as suggested in the completion exercise, for the most part isn't true for me. These likes and dislikes are just fear, a manifestation of my panic disorder. They are no more me than a list of food allergies to avoid.

I assume I should just keep going even though my experience of having a personality and developing into my own person didn't happen until I became an adult and slowly started trying to figure what happened at home?

3. My hymn to Johnny Appleseed will be published in AODA's 2023 Druid Book. A fellow member helped by putting it in a musical score software. Once I find my higher-quality microphone, I will upload a recording and share it. Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Late/Incomplete Personality Development & Dolmen Arch's "Who is Kim?"
replies.content:
1) You're most welcome.

2) No system of exercises can be perfectly suited to everyone. I'd encourage you to keep working with it for a couple of weeks, but accept that your experience with it will be different from most.

3) Congratulations! That's good to hear.

comments.title: Atoms, cosmic and otherwise
comments.content:
Thank you for your answer in the CosDoc-commentary-announcement thread. I guess I've taken it too literally once more - so when I am not aware of Cosmic things this just means that I have not developed the capacity to directly react to cosmic influences? When I am "just some kind of movement" and the cosmic things are another kind of movement, these movements are not compatible in such way that their way of moving can't be perceived by me?

If I may, I have another point in the text that confuses me *slightly* (actually many, but this is a larger one): Fortune writes on p. 80 (millennium edition), that traveling atoms are not stable and can therefore revert to a primitive state after reaching a highly evolved state. Well, this sounds a little bit prototypical to reincarnation? Or is it meant that they can adapt to the less complex conditions on a given plane which seems to be hinted by the remark that "they can't be changed by those primitive forces as they can act on the atoms of their plane"?

And then - last for today - I wondered whether the (circular) rhythmic pattern that is imprinted to the (cosmic) atom by the logos and allows the corresponding traveling atom to be "more alive" than the other stationary atoms and to gather matter around it - is this the principle of developing a mental body? You try to form a vortex on the mental plane by some kind of rhythmical/circular actions that allow you to aggregate mental plane matter and build a body out of it? And possibly closely related I wonder whether the practice of consecrating a lingam (in the Hindu tradition) is some kind of "animating dead matter"? I don't know very much about this but from what I've heard, the lingams are treated as a conscious being which has a material and subtle bodies.

I'm overflowing with questions and many things seem to be very opaque right now... There seems to be a complexity-explosion, not only in the cosmos Fortune describes :-)

Greetings,
Nachtgurke

replies.title: Re: Atoms, cosmic and otherwise
replies.content: I'm going to encourage you to write down every question that comes to mind as you study the text and commentary, and then meditate on every single one of those questions. Since this is intended to train the mind, not to inform it, the exercise you'll get by exploring these questions yourself will be worth far more to you than any answer I can give you.

comments.title: Premonitions of death
comments.content: I'm freaking out a bit. 2 weeks ago I asked (anonymously) whether anyone else was experiencing feelings of extreme dread. I thought it had to do with the general worldwide times we're having. But it turns out to be more specific to me. Two premonitions of two deaths, my next door neighbour, with my friend who had committed suicide trying to warn me in a dream and again another way, and now a close friend whose passing I 'knew'before it was confirmed to me today. My mother used to get these premonitions and it was hellish for her. It's distressing to me too. Is this a side effect of magic practice? I can't stop now, my basic practices are keeping me sane. Any advice on dealing with this? Or will it pass?

replies.title: Re: Premonitions of death
replies.content: Ouch. No, it's probably hereditary -- such things run in families. It may be more intense right now because the feelings of dread are pretty widespread just now, and for good reason. As for how best to deal with it, I wish I knew.

replies.title: Re: Premonitions of death
replies.content: This sounds more intense than what happens to me but for a while there I was seeing too many synchronicities. What I did in a moment of distress was just decree to my mind: "Look, sometimes I just don't want to know and I am the one in charge of deciding when to open and close the psychic senses. *stumped on the ground with my right foot*; turned 180 degrees and walked the other way to my destiny at the time thinking about something else. I did this a few times. It did improve things for me, though closing completely doesn't seem to be an option for me anymore either because of continued occult practice as you mention.

replies.title: Re: Premonitions of death
replies.content:
I have a friend and a family member with similar sensitivity and awareness. One of the things that caused them anxiety was worry about every thought and feeling that came up and trying to discern the intuitions from the imaginings. One of the techniques we came up with together was to sit with any new info and trace where it came from. For example, a feeling that a loved one would be in a vehicle accident came directly down a thread of thought from knowing the friend was traveling and seeing news of bad weather and car accidents, so it was dismissed as a thought rather than an intuition and then the stress it caused could be breathed away. On the other hand a new piece of info that came unbidden with no discernible train of thought would be taken seriously.

For the pieces of info that are deemed intuitions, perhaps it would help to work the opposite direction - if you have a train of thought leading forward from the intuition then there may be some action to take, from something as simple as prayer or blessing, to as complex as interacting with a human about it. If there's nothing clear to do, perhaps try to treat it simply as if you heard the info from someone. A big stress is that it media has trained us that capabilities like this are a superpower you are supposed to do something with. As far as I understand, it rarely is, it's just reading the news a day or two early.

Intending you wellness and balance.

Justin Patrick Moore
Turquoise Fuzzy Grasshopper

replies.title: Re: GSF - Occult Philosophy
replies.content:
Congratulations, initiate of the Fellowship!

1) Remember that you can always spend more than a year on the book. There are no prizes for completing the work under a certain time limit! If you want, you can spend two weeks on each chapter, making room for a scrying, a series of meditations on the scrying, and some additional meditations on the themes of the lesson.

2) You can do that if you want to, but you certainly don't have to.

Once I finish responding to this round of comments, btw, I'm going to get back to work on the next volume in the GSF series -- The Earth Mysteries Workbook.

replies.title: Re: GSF - Occult Philosophy
replies.content:
Hey John,

Thanks for your measured response. I need to remember: "There are no prizes for completing the work under a certain time limit!"

When I first read it this morning, it gave me this idea: that if I spread these meditations out over approx. two years, then I will have time to do the whole series of scryings on the 33 emblems, which would be really good to do. Beyond that, it would also give me the opportunity to do some further work on incorporating the extended techniques of the SOP from the DMH into this work. I would probably keep the seasonal meditations to about week, and coordinating those with the FHR equinox & solstice rituals in the open lodge, and some extra time to work on the MOE material in the afternoons. In any case my first meditation on the visible & invisible went well today. A lot to unpack. So all-in-all I'll have plenty to keep me occupied with occult & magical work for the coming two years, and hopefully keep a good pace without trying to race.

Justin Patrick Moore
Persimmon Officious Centaur
